segments.s371-g57: سپه را همه: 'سپه' (the army) is marked by the object marker 'را' and modified by 'همه' (the whole/everyone). 'پیش رفتن دهم' is an older Persian construction where the infinitive 'رفتن' (to go) is followed by the 1st‑person verb 'دهم' to mean 'I will cause/send (to) go' (a causative/let construction). Word order places the object phrase before the verb cluster, typical of epic diction.
segments.s371-g58: ورا = 'to him' (dative/indirect object). 'خسروی تاج' is an adjective + noun sequence meaning 'the kingly/royal crown.' 'بر سر نهم' = 'I place on the head'; 'نهم' is 1st person singular (from نهادن/نِهَستن in classical usage) meaning 'I put/place' (here future/intentive). The prepositional phrase 'بر سر' ('upon the head') follows and completes the action.
segments.s371-g59: چنانچون = 'just as' (subordinating/comparative). The clause 'پدر داد شاهی مرا' reads subject-verb-object + clitic: 'پدر' (father) is subject, 'داد' is the past tense 'gave', 'شاهی' = 'kingship' is the thing given, and 'مرا' (here split in the tokenization as 'م' + 'را') is the accusative/dative clitic 'to me'. This is a simple past clause meaning 'my father gave kingship to me.'
segments.s371-g60: 'دهم' is 1st person singular (present/optative/future sense) 'I give / I will give.' 'همچنان' modifies the verb and means 'in the same way' or 'likewise.' 'پادشاهی ورا' = 'the kingship to him' (object then dative clitic). Word order here places the verb before its object for emphasis, a common rhetorical/artistic order in Ferdowsi.
segments.s371-g33: کرا is an archaic relative meaning 'who/whom' placed at the front for emphasis. کشت (here 'killing') with خواهد forms a periphrastic future/causative idea — 'will be killed' or 'will slay' — and همی is an archaic intensifying/continuous particle that often appears around verbs in poetry. روزگار ('Fate') is the agent placed after the verb phrase (inverted, poetic word order): literally 'whom Fate will kill'.
segments.s371-g34: چه is an exclamatory 'what/how' introducing the rhetorical question. نیکوتر is the comparative 'better' (more noble). از is the comparative preposition 'than' (poetic use of از). مرگ در کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'death in/at battle' (در = 'in/at'), so the clause reads 'what is better than death in battle'.
segments.s371-g35: بدانید is the (plural/formal) imperative 'know' (from دانستن). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the verb. که introduces the subordinate content clause. روزیست is a predicative expression meaning 'this is a matter of fate / it is ordained' (روزی + است fused in poetic language), and این is the demonstrative 'this' emphasizing that predicate; the order is somewhat inverted for emphasis.
segments.s371-g36: The second که begins the specific content of what is 'rozi' (fated). کافر is 'an infidel' (the noun subject). پدید آید is a two-part verbal idea: پدید (appear/come forth) plus آید ('comes' / 'happens'); together 'shall appear' or 'will arise'. از پاک دین is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from a pure religion' describing the origin of that appearance.
segments.s371-g37: شما is the polite/plural 'you' (subject). از پس پشتها is an idiomatic/poetic locative phrase 'from behind the backs / over (your) shoulders' (پس‌پشت = 'rear/behind'). منگرید is a prohibitive/negative form 'do not look' (2nd person plural), so the clause commands 'do not look back over your shoulders.'
segments.s371-g38: مجویید is the prohibitive plural 'do not seek' or 'do not call for' (imperative negative). فریاد is a direct object 'a cry/shout' — 'do not cry out (for help)'. و simply links the two prohibitions. سر is 'head' and مشمرید is an archaic/obscure verbal form; in context it functions as a second prohibition with سر as its object or adverbial (roughly 'do not let your head droop / do not cower'). (مشمرید is an obsolete form and its exact nuance is uncertain.)
segments.s371-g39: نگر is an imperative 'look/keep watch' (poetic imperative). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'. نبینید is the negative subjunctive/imperative 'you should not see / do not see'. بگریختن is an infinitive/verbal noun 'to flee' or 'fleeing' serving as the content of what one must not behold — 'watch so that you do not see (any) fleeing.'
segments.s371-g40: Again نگر is the imperative 'watch'. تا gives purpose 'so that'. نترسید is the prohibitive/negative exhortation 'do not fear'. ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' and here introduces the gerund آویختن ('hanging' — i.e., being hanged or hanging), so the clause means 'watch so that you do not fear hanging.'
segments.s371-g25: همه (subject) + ایستادند (past plural verb) is a simple S-V clause: “everyone stood.” The prepositional phrase در پیش اوی follows the verb and specifies location/in-presence: در پیش = ‘before/in the presence of’. اوی is an archaic/pronounced form of او used here as a third-person object/locative ‘him’.
segments.s371-g26: که is a conjunction giving a reason (‘for/that’). لشکر is the noun ‘army’ and شکستن is the infinitive/masdar ‘breaking’ used adjectivally/purposively (the army whose purpose/role is to break). بدی modifies کیش as ‘evil’ + ‘kish’ = ‘evil creed/custom’, and اوی again marks possession/association ‘his’. Together the phrase gives the reason: ‘for his army was to break the evil creed.’
segments.s371-g27: به marks the indirect object/recipient (‘to/for’). آزادگان is the addressee plural ‘the freemen / nobles’. گفت is the verb ‘said/spoke’ (past) and پیش سپاه is a locative phrase ‘in front of the army / before the host’ — word order here places the recipient and verb before the locative phrase.
segments.s371-g28: که‌ای functions as a vocative/exclamatory opening (classical combination of که + ای, effectively ‘O’). نامداران is plural ‘renowned ones’ (vocative), و links items, گردان here means ‘troop/battalion’, and شاه is in apposition/genitive relation ‘of the king’ — together a direct address: ‘O renowned men and battalions of the king.’
segments.s371-g29: نگر is an imperative interjection ‘look!/listen!/consider!’. تا here introduces purpose/result (‘so that’). چه گویم is an embedded clause ‘what I shall say’ with گویم as a first‑person present/subjunctive used to indicate the utterance to come. یکی بشنوید is an exhortation: یکی here functions as a distributive/vocative particle (roughly ‘one/each’ or ‘now’ in classical usage) and بشنوید is 2nd‑person‑plural imperative/subjunctive ‘hear/listen’.
segments.s371-g30: به marks the target/direction ‘to/into’. دین is the direct object ‘religion/faith’. خدای جهان is a possessive/genitive phrase ‘the God (lord) of the world’. بگروید is the 2nd‑person‑plural jussive/subjunctive/imperative of گرویدن ‘to adopt/embrace’ and here is an exhortation: ‘adopt the faith of the Lord of the world.’
segments.s371-g31: نگر again is the imperative ‘consider/see’. تا introduces purpose/result (‘so that’). نترسید is the negative present/imperative 2nd‑person‑plural ‘do not fear’. از selects the source/object of fear (‘of/from’), مرگ is ‘death’, and و چیز (‘and a thing’) functions idiomatically to mean ‘or anything’. The clause therefore reads: ‘Consider, so that you do not fear death or anything.’
segments.s371-g32: که is a causal/explanatory conjunction ‘for/because’. کس here means ‘no one’ in the negative sense determined by the verb that follows. بی‌زمانه is an adjective ‘untimely’ (not at the appointed time). نمردست is an archaic/past construction meaning ‘has not died / did not die before his time’ (the listed lemma senses do not match this usage, see note). نیز is a final particle ‘also/indeed’. The clause gives the reassurance: ‘for no one has died untimely (before his time).’
segments.s371-g49: به دین خدا is an oath formula where به functions as an adverbial 'by' (used in swearing) and دین خدا = 'God's religion/God' stands as the thing sworn by. گو is the imperative 'say/tell' and takes the following proper name as its object: اسفندیار = 'Esfandiyar' (poetic word order places the verb before its object). The short exclamation-like form خدا‌ای here is part of the oath and reads as 'by God'.
segments.s371-g50: به جان زریر is a second oath formula: به = 'by', جان = 'life/soul', زریر = the proper name Zarir, so 'by Zarir's life'. آن modifies سوار ('that rider'), and نبرده is a poetic past negative form 'has not taken/carried off' (literal verb precedes its noun in this inversion common in the epic). Together the clause asserts that 'that rider has not carried him off.'
segments.s371-g51: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('that/so that'). آید here is a present/subjunctive-like verb 'may come' or 'will come', and فرود او ('his descent/entrance') uses the noun فرود with the pronoun او ('his') following it — a poetic noun+possessor order. کنون 'now' and در بهشت 'into/in Paradise' locate the action temporally and spatially: 'may his entrance come now into Paradise.'
segments.s371-g52: که again links this clause (here giving the reason): 'for/since'. من is the first‑person pronoun 'I'. سوی + لهراسپ marks the destination/recipient 'to Lohrasp' (سوی = 'toward/to'). نامه نوشت is the verb phrase 'wrote a letter' with نوشت as the past tense verb 'wrote' placed after its object in poetic word order.
segments.s371-g53: پذیرفتم is the first‑person past tense 'I accepted'. اندرز ('advice/counsel') is the direct object placed after the verb (poetic inversion). آن شاه پیر = 'that old king' (آن = 'that', شاه = 'king', پیر = 'old'), specifying whose counsel was accepted.
segments.s371-g54: که‌گر is the fused archaic conditional 'که گر' = 'if'. بخت نیکم = 'my good fortune' (بخت = 'fate/fortune', نیکم here = 'good' with the possessive implication). بود is the past tense copula 'were/was' (here counterfactual), and دستگیر is a predicative adjective 'helping/one who comes to the rescue' (i.e. 'he would have been a helper'). The clause expresses a hypothetical: 'if my fortune had been good, he would be/been my helper.'
segments.s371-g55: که introduces the subordinate temporal clause ('that/so that' or linking clause). چون is used here with temporal value 'when' (in archaic/poetic usage). بازگردم is the first‑person verb 'I return' (present/subjunctive used for future time), and ازین رزمگاه = 'from this battlefield' (ازین = 'from this', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before its locative complement).
segments.s371-g56: به here is the preposition 'to/for' marking the beneficiary. اسفندیارم is the proper name Esfandiyar with an enclitic (older poetic morphology) indicating the personal object/relation ('to Esfandiyar' / 'my Esfandiyar' in effect). دهم is the first‑person present/subjunctive often used for future 'I will give'. تاج و گاه = 'crown and gāh' — تاج = 'crown', گاه in this context denotes the seat/kingship (here 'the kingship/throne'), so 'I will give Esfandiyar the crown and the kingship.'
segments.s371-g9: کی here is temporal 'when' (classical use). نامور is an epithet 'the famed/renowned one' placed before the action. دست بر دست زد is a literal sequence 'strike hand on hand' (دست + بر + دست + زد) with زد in the simple past 3rd singular; in context it describes striking/clapping the hands (a gesture of grief).
segments.s371-g10: بنالید is past (3rd singular) 'he lamented/moaned'. ازان + روزگاران بد is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'of those/because of those bad days' (ازان = 'of/from that', روزگاران = 'times/days', بد = 'bad'); the whole phrase gives the object/cause of the lament.
segments.s371-g11: همه‌ساله is an adverbial phrase 'every year'. زین روز in classical Persian is a literary 'from this/that day' (زین = 'from this/that' in older usage) marking the temporal starting point. ترسیدمی is an archaic first-person past form ('I feared' / 'I used to fear') with the older -می suffix that occurs frequently in epic style.
segments.s371-g12: چو = 'when'. او را places 'him' as a definite object/topic (را is the object marker). به رزم اندرون is a locational phrase 'into/in the midst of battle' (به = toward/into, رزم = battle, اندرون = inside/within). دیدمی is the speaker's past 'I saw' in the same archaic -می form as above.
segments.s371-g13: دریغا is an exclamatory interjection 'Alas!'. سوارا is the noun 'rider/horseman' with the classical object/definite ending -ا (here used in exclamation/accusative/attention). گوا is a particle 'it seems/appears' (marks reported appearance or attestation) and links the following name as apposition. مهترا is a proper name in apposition to the rider.
segments.s371-g14: که introduces a relative/cause clause 'who/that'. بختش is 'his fate/fortune' (possessive -ش). جدا کرد is a compound idea 'separated/removed' with جدا (apart) + کرد (past of کردن 'to do/bring about') — 'caused to be removed'. تاج از سرا = 'the crown from (his) head' where از marks source; سرا here is a poetic/archaic form used for the head (poetic use rather than 'house').
segments.s371-g15: که again introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. کشت is the simple past 'killed' (transitive). آن is the demonstrative 'that'. سیه modifies پیل as 'black/dark'. نستوه is an adjectival epithet attached to the elephant; its exact shade of meaning is archaic/poetic (context: a strong/remarkable elephant) and does not match the modern sense-list provided. را is the object marker confirming the elephant as the object of کشت.
segments.s371-g16: که continues the relative idea. کند here is the classical/presentive form of کردن 'to do/make' used in a descriptive/relative sense (here best read as 'who tore/removed' or 'who could pull up'); از marks source 'from the earth'. زمین آهنین 'iron earth' = 'the iron-like ground' (آهنین modifies زمین). کوه را ('the mountain' + object marker را) is the object being taken from the earth; the poetic word order and classical verb form produce the image 'who tore the iron mountain from the earth'.
segments.s371-g41: 'سر نیزه‌ها را' = 'the heads of the spears' (سر = top/head + نیزه‌ها = spears) with را marking the definite direct object. 'به رزم' is a prepositional phrase 'for/into battle'. 'افگنید' is an archaic/poetic verb form (imperative/optative plural) meaning 'set/throw/put' (here: 'set/cast into battle'). Word order: object + prepositional goal + verb (verb at end) as in classical Persian.
segments.s371-g42: 'زمانی' is an adverbial/time word 'at that time/now' (temporal). 'بکوشید' is a second‑person plural imperative/subjunctive 'strive/try'. The conjunction 'و' links the clauses. 'مردی کنید' is an idiomatic predicate: 'مردی' = 'manliness/courage' and 'کنید' (here causative/performative) = 'do/show'; together 'show manliness' or 'act like men'.
segments.s371-g43: 'بدین' (rendered here as ب + دین in the word list) functions adverbially 'thus / in this way'. 'اندرون بود' = 'was inside' (اندرون = inside; بود = past copula 'was'). The proper name 'اسفندیار' is the subject; classical Persian often places the verb before the subject.
segments.s371-g44: The subordinating conjunction 'که' here introduces the event (≈ 'when' / 'that'): 'بانگ پدرش آمد از کوهسار' = 'the cry/shout of his father came from the mountain'. 'بانگ' = shout/voice, 'پدرش' = 'his father' (پدر + suffix ش = his), 'آمد' = 'came', 'از کوهسار' = 'from the mountainside'.
segments.s371-g45: که‌ای here is an archaic vocative combination (که + ای) used to call out to people: “O …”. نامداران و گردان من = “the renowned ones and my troop(s)”; گردان is a collective noun meaning a body of warriors, and من is the possessor (“my”). Word order places the vocative particle before the addressees.
segments.s371-g46: همه = “all/everyone” (subject). مر (dative) + مرا (accusative/emphatic) is a classical doubled-pronoun construction meaning “to me”/“for me” for emphasis; م (here a contracted form of هست) is the copular verb “is/are”. را functions as the object/ topical marker attached to همه. چون = “like/as”; تن و جان من = “my body and my soul” (چون introduces the comparison). Overall: 'همه مر مرا م(هست) را چون تن و جان من' = 'all of you are to me as my body and soul.'
segments.s371-g47: مترسید is an archaic 2nd-person plural negative imperative/form of بیم داشتن here meaning “do not fear.” از + list of weapons (نیزه و گرز و تیغ) marks the objects of fear: “of/from the spear, the mace, and the sword.” The conjunctions و simply join the items in the list.
segments.s371-g48: که is a causal conjunction here (“for/because”). از بخش‌مان = “from/part of our portion” (بخش = lot, portion; -مان = our). نیست = copula negation “is not.” روی = “face/countenance,” and گریغ is an adjective meaning “sullen, haughty” (i.e. proud or sulky bearing). Together: “for a sullen/proud countenance is not in our lot.”
segments.s371-g1: پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. آگاهی 'information/news' is the subject and آمد is the past verb 'came' (literal 'information came'); به + name marks the indirect object/dative 'to Esfandiyar'. Word order is classical Persian: (temporal) + subject + verb + indirect object.
segments.s371-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. کشته شد is a passive perfect construction 'was killed' (کشته = 'killed/slain' + شد as auxiliary 'became/was'). آن شاه نیزه گزار is a definite noun phrase 'that/the king, the spear‑bearer' where نیزه گزار is an epithet ('spear‑bearer') applied to شاه.
segments.s371-g3: پدرت is پدر 'father' + second‑person suffix -ت = 'your father' (subject). از غم او = 'from/of his grief' (a prepositional phrase indicating the sorrow involved). بکاهد is the classical present/future form of کاهیدن 'to lessen' (ب‑prefix plus present stem) and همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle often marking ongoing or habitual action; together convey 'will lessen/relieve'.
segments.s371-g4: کنون = 'now'. کین او = 'his vengeance/hatred' (کین with possessor او). خواست here functions as the noun/Verbal‑noun 'desire/will' and خواهد is the future/modal auxiliary 'will' — classical word order places the verbal noun before the auxiliary (خاستْ خواهد → 'will desire/seek'); همی again adds continuous/ emphatic sense. Overall: 'now he will (come to) desire/seek his vengeance.'
segments.s371-g5: همی گوید = 'one keeps saying' or 'people say' (همی marks repeated/continuous speech; گوید is the archaic/present form 'says'). آنکس = 'that person' / 'one'. کجاکین is a fused interrogative phrase roughly 'from where/where will (the) kīn (vengeance) come?' and اوی is a possessive/third‑person clitic 'his' (so 'کین اوی' = 'his vengeance'). The word order is compressed and poetic: 'one says (that): where will his vengeance come from?'
segments.s371-g6: بخواهد is the modal/future 'will' (or 'would'), نهد is the present infinitive/3rd‑person form of نهاد 'to place/set' meaning here 'to set/put'. پیش دشمنش = 'before/in front of his enemy' (پیش = before/in presence of; دشمنش = enemy + his suffix). روی 'face' is the direct object: 'to set his face before his enemy' i.e. 'to confront his enemy'.
segments.s371-g7: مر is an archaic/emphatic first‑person particle ('for me' / 'I will' / 'me') that introduces the speaker's action. او را is the indirect object 'to him' (او + object marker را). دهم (understood in the clause) is the first‑person singular present/future 'I give / I will give'. دخترم را 'my daughter' is the direct object (دختر + possessive -م + object marker را). همای is the proper name 'Homay' (the daughter). The clause thus says 'I will give him my daughter Homay.'
segments.s371-g8: وکرد = و + کرد 'and did/made' (past). ایزدش = ایزد 'god/deity' + possessive ش 'his' (literally 'his god' or 'may his deity/fortune'); را marks the object. برین بر گوای is a compressed classical phrase: بر این (بر + این, here written برین) = 'on/for this', and گوای (from گوا/گواهـ) = 'witness' — so the whole phrase means 'and made his god witness to this' (i.e. invoked the deity as witness).
segments.s371-g17: A nominal list of objects placed before the verb (typical Persian word order in poetry): درفش (the standard), سرلشکر (the army‑commander) and جای خویش (his own post/position). The conjunction و links the nouns in a simple enumerative series; خویش is formed from خوی + possessive ش ('his'), yielding 'his own'.
segments.s371-g18: برادرش را داد is a complete verb phrase: را marks برادرش as the definite object and داد is the past tense 'gave' (subject omitted, understood 'he'). The following و خود رفت پیش is an independent clause: خود is emphatic 'he himself' and رفت (past of رفتن) + پیش ('forward/in front') => 'he himself went forward'.
segments.s371-g19: به قلب اندر آمد is a locative phrase + verb: به ... آمد (came to/into) with قلب 'the heart/center' and اندر 'in/within' emphasizing movement into the midst. به جای زریر (‘into Zarīr’s place’) indicates he occupied Zarīr's position; جای here means 'post/position'.
segments.s371-g20: به صف اندر استاد: به صف اندر (into the battle‑line) + استاد which in this poetic context is an archaic past form meaning 'stood' (from ایستادن), so the clause means 'he stood in the ranks'. چون introduces a simile: چون نره شیر = 'like a male lion' (نره qualifying شیر).
segments.s371-g21: به پیش اندر آمد = 'he came forward' (به + locative پیش + اندر آمد). میان را ببست: میان (the middle/gap) is the definite object marked by را and ببست (past) = 'he closed/blocked' — object‑before‑verb ordering is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s371-g22: گرفت is the past 'he seized/took'; آن درفش همایون is the object phrase 'that auspicious/royal standard' (همایون a royal epithet). به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in (his) hand' — literal ordering 'took that standard royal into (his) hand'.
segments.s371-g23: برادرش is the subject ('his brother'). بد here means 'bad/ill' (qualifying the situation); دانسته is a past participle 'having known' or 'perceiving/realizing', and راه is the object 'the way/path' (i.e. the course of action). The exact function of پنج in the line is uncertain in this printed segmentation (it appears in the text but is not given as a separate token); possibilities are a numeral inserted for emphasis or part of an idiom. Grammatically this segment reads as 'his brother, having perceived matters to be bad, knew/took the way'.
segments.s371-g24: همه 'all/everyone' + از در تاج 'from the gate of the crown' (در here = 'gate/portal' rather than merely the preposition) + و همتای شاه 'and the king's peers/counterparts'. از marks origin ('from'); the phrase lists those who come/from where — 'all, from the royal gate and the king's peers'.

sentences.s371-s29: I will send the whole army ahead of him and place the royal crown upon his head.
sentences.s371-s30: Just as my father gave me the kingship, so will I likewise give the kingship to him.
sentences.s371-s17: Whomsoever Fate will slay — what could be better than death on the battlefield?
sentences.s371-s18: Know fully that this is fate — that an infidel will arise out of a pure religion.
sentences.s371-s19: You: do not look back over your shoulders; do not call for help nor let your head droop.
sentences.s371-s20: Keep watch so that you do not see anyone flee; keep watch so that you do not fear being hanged.
sentences.s371-s13: Everyone stood before him, for his army was set to break the evil creed.
sentences.s371-s14: He addressed the freemen before the host, saying, “O famed men and the king’s battalions.”
sentences.s371-s15: Listen, so that you may hear what I shall say; adopt the religion of the God of the world.
sentences.s371-s16: Consider, so that you do not fear death or anything, for no one dies untimely.
sentences.s371-s25: Tell Esfandiyar, ‘By God — by Zarir's life, that mounted man has not carried him off.’
sentences.s371-s26: That his descent may now come into Paradise; for I wrote a letter to Lohrasp.
sentences.s371-s27: I accepted the counsel of that old king; for if my fortune had been good, he would have been my helper.
sentences.s371-s28: That when I return from this battlefield I will give Esfandiyar the crown and the kingship.
sentences.s371-s5: When the famed man struck his hands together, he lamented those evil days.
sentences.s371-s6: Every year, from that day I feared, when I saw him within the battle.
sentences.s371-s7: Alas! the rider — it seems, Mahtara — for whose fate the crown was taken from his head.
sentences.s371-s8: — the one who killed that black elephant (nestooh) — who tore the iron mountain from the earth.
sentences.s371-s21: Set the spear‑heads for battle; strive when the time comes and show manly courage.
sentences.s371-s22: Thus Esfandiyar was inside, when his father's cry came from the mountain.
sentences.s371-s23: O famed men and my troops, all of you are to me like my body and my soul.
sentences.s371-s24: Do not fear the spear, the mace, or the sword; for a sullen (proud) face is not in our portion.
sentences.s371-s1: Then news came to Esfandiyar that that spear‑bearing king had been killed.
sentences.s371-s2: Your father will ease his sorrow over him; now he will seek (or desire) that man's vengeance.
sentences.s371-s3: People keep saying, 'Where will he find the will (or hatred) to set his face before his enemy?';
sentences.s371-s4: I will give him my daughter Homay, and made his god witness to this.
sentences.s371-s9: He gave the standard, the commander, and his own post to his brother, and he himself moved forward.
sentences.s371-s10: He came into the center, taking Zarīr's place; he stood in the line like a male lion.
sentences.s371-s11: He came forward and closed the middle, seizing that royal standard in his hand.
sentences.s371-s12: His brother, seeing that things were ill, knew the way (took action); all came from the royal gate and the king's peers.
